content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 630 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 6, 2017

Rosann Gallien
Deputy General Counsel
San Diego County Water Authority
4677 Overland Avenue
San Diego, CA 92123-1233

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-263

Dear Ms. Gallien:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Jerry Butkiewicz, Board member of the San Diego County Water Authority ("Water Authority"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090, et seq.1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. Nor do we advise under other areas of law that could be affected, including restrictions on incompatible activities. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) The following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may Mr. Butkiewicz participate in Water Authority contract decisions regarding San Diego Gas & Electric ("SDG&E"), if he accepts employment as a contract consultant for SDG&E's independent marketing division, Sempra Services Corporation ("SSC")?

2. Would Section 1090 prohibit the Water Authority from entering into contracts with SDG&E if Mr. Butkiewicz accepts employment as a consultant to SSC?

_________________________

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Under the Act, if Water Authority Board member Jerry Butkiewicz accepts
employment with SSC, he a conflict of interest and may not participate in the making of any
contracts with SDG&E.

2. Yes. In the event he becomes a contract consultant to SSC, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a
financial interest in contracts with SDG&E under Section 1090, and the Water Authority would be
barred from making further contracts or negotiating existing contracts with SDG&E.

FACTS

You provided the Public Utility's Draft Resolution E-4874, dated July 14, 2016 for
background on the issue of SDG&E's Independent Marketing Division, SSC.

In 2011 the Legislature added Public Utilities Code Section 707, which requires that an
electrical corporation shall only market against a community choice aggregation program through a
pre-approved independent marketing division.² This independent marketing division must be
"funded exclusively by the electrical corporation's shareholders" and physically and functionally
separate from the electrical corporation's ratepayer-funded divisions. (Public Utilities Commission
("PUC") Agenda ID 14972, Draft Resolution E-4874, July 14, 2016, page 2.)

SDG&E³ is the first electrical corporation to request the PUC's approval in establishing an
independent marketing division. (PUC Agenda, supra, page 2.) The PUC notes that SDG&E chose
to locate its independent marketing division inside an already existing affiliate, SSC.⁴ The purpose
of SSC is to market and lobby against community choice aggregators ("CCAs")⁵ in the San Diego
area on behalf of SDG&E. (PUC Agenda, supra page 4.) In its approval, the PUC ordered that SSC
be funded "entirely by shareholders" of SDG&E. (PUC Agenda, supra, page 17.) SDG&E is
required to file reports with the PUC Energy Division detailing its spending and shareholder-
funding of SSC. (Id., p. 18.)⁶

² Senate Bill 790, c. 599 of 2011.

³ SDG&E is a wholly owned subsidiary of Sempra Energy, a California based Fortune 500 company. Board
member Butkiewicz is a former employee of SDG&E. Sempra Energy is the main provider of gas and electric services
in the San Diego region.

⁴ Under the PUC's applicable "Affiliate Transaction Rules" an "affiliate is defined as a corporation "5 per cent
or more of whose outstanding securities are owned, controlled, or held with power to vote, directly or indirectly either
by a utility or any of its subsidiaries, or by that utility's controlling corporation and/or any of its subsidiaries as well as
any company in which the utility, its controlling corporation, or any of the utility's affiliates exert substantial control
over the operation of the company and/or indirectly have substantial financial interests in the company exercised
through means other than ownership . . ." (Affiliate Transaction Rules Applicable to Large California Energy Utilities.,
1. Definitions, page 1, Decision 06-12-029.)

⁵ These are governmental entities formed by cities and counties to serve the energy requirements of their local
residents and businesses.

⁶ Additionally, the PUC determined that SSC is subject to all the "Affiliate Transaction Rules," which set forth
separation requirements related to operations, information, financial books, facilities, and protection of non-public

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 3

Recently appointed Water Authority Board member Jerry Butkiewicz is considering
employment as a contract consultant with SSC, the independent marketing division of SDG&E. The
Water Authority is a public agency which does business with SDG&E on energy and conservation
matters. The Water Authority regularly enters into contracts with SDG&E for power purchase
agreements, conservation and rebate agreements, grants of easements, and other matters related to
energy and water. The Water Authority does not have any contracts with SSC, and does not
anticipate any such agreements.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest under the Act

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's
interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can
give rise to a conflict of interest; the ones most relevant to the facts in this situation are:

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public
official within 12 months before the decision is made.

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family.

Source of Income & Personal Finances

SSC, as is required of an independent marketing division by P.U. Code Section 707, is
funded wholly by SDG&E through its shareholder funds. Therefore, the source of any income Mr.
Butkiewicz receives as a SSC consultant is SDG&E. He will have a financial interest in SSC and
SDG&E as a source of income once he receives, or is promised to receive, $500 as a consultant for
SSC and SDG&E, within the twelve months of a decision. (Regulation 18700.1(a)(1).) Mr.
Butkiewicz also has an interest in his personal finances.

utility information. (PUC Agenda ID 14972, supra, page 14.) Under these rules, SSC and SDG&E may not share
support employees engaged in marketing or lobbying, but may share joint corporate oversight, governance, support
systems, and support personnel engaged in marketing or lobbying. (Id., page 14 and Appendix, page 4.) The PUC
further directed SDG&E to ensure that "their consultants or contractors working on Independent Marketing Division
matters comply with the Code of Conduct . . . and Affiliate Transaction Rules" and not in any way act as conduct to
allow SDG&E or the Independent Marketing Division to circumvent these rules. (Id., page 18, item 6.)

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 4

Foreseeability: A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed "reasonably
foreseeable" if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision as a named party in, or subject of,
the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Where SDG&E is a named party to the contracts at issue, it is
therefore reasonably foreseeable that any contract decision will have a financial impact on SDG&E,
Mr. Butkiewicz's source of income.

Materiality: Business Entity as Source of Income

Regulation 18702.1 provides the standard for materiality when the income is for services or
a salary and the source is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation
18702.1(a), as relevant to these facts, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is
material if the business entity:

(2) Offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's agency; or

(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency;

Your question involves written contracts with SDG&E for services to the Water Authority,
. and therefore the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is material.

In the event that Mr. Butkiewicz becomes a contract consultant to SSC, he will have a
prohibited financial interest in any contract decisions between the Water Authority and SDG&E.
Mr. Butkiewicz would need to comply with the procedures for disqualification set forth in Section
87105 as to making, participating in making or influencing any governmental decision related to
contracts with SDG&E, as detailed below.

Making a Decision:

An official is "making a decision" if the official "authorizes or directs any action, votes,
appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any
contractual agreement on behalf of his agency." (Regulation 18704(a).) Participating in a decision
occurs when the official "provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of
affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Regulation 18704(b).)
Lastly, an official uses his or her position to influence a decision if the official contacts or appears
before any official in his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision. (Regulation
18704(c).)

In addition, when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including
public officials who manage public investments or public funds) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a
public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally
identify each type of financial interest involved in the decision as well as details of the financial
interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 5

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate and distinct from that applied under the Act. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)

As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. However, the Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest" and involves a public body or board as defined in Section 1091, or is deemed a "noninterest" as defined in Section 1091.5.

Where a remote interest is present, the contract may be lawfully executed provided: (1) the officer discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is noted in the public body's official records; and (3) the officer completely abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091.)

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a public officer or employee; (2) involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or non-interest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply?

Steps one and two are not at issue here. This matter involves a public officer and a contract. As no facts have been alleged regarding step six, we do not consider the rule of necessity. We focus on steps three, four and five.

Step Three: Is the Official Making or Participating in Making a Contract?

Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, for purposes of Section 1090, participating in making a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall, supra.)

Additionally, when an official is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract, he or she is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 6

contract. (Thomson, supra, pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 49 (2006).)

Mr. Butkiewicz, as a member of the Water Authority Board, is conclusively presumed to be
involved in the making of contracts between the Water Authority and SDG&E.

Step Four: Does the Official Have a Financial Interest in Contracts with SDG&E?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest" (People v. Honig, (1996) 48 Cal. App. 4th 289, 333), and officials are deemed to
have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Id.) Although Section
1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General
Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve
financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson,
supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Terry
v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-
432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002).) Employees have been found to have a financial
interest in a contract that involves their employer, even where the contract would not result in a
change in income or directly involve the employee, because an employee has an overall interest in
the financial success of the firm and continued employment. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162
(2001).)

Similar to the financial interest of an employee, past advice letters have found that an
independent contractor has a financial interest in continuing the business relationship and looking
for future business opportunities with a contracting firm. (Roy Advice Letter, No. A-16-157, see
also Khuu Advice Letter, No. I-14-107.) In Roy, we found that a county Health and Human Services
Agency public official would have a financial interest in a contract between the county and a
hospital if she took an independent contractor, temporary physician position, at a medical group.
Although the medical group would pay her under the contract, the medical group's costs were
reimbursed by the hospital. Referring to reasoning from past advice letters and Attorney General
Opinions, the Roy Advice letter found that even where the payment for services came indirectly
from the hospital, the public official's relationship could be "influenced by a desire to maintain a
favorable ongoing relationship" with the hospital and "the prospect of future business
opportunities." (Roy Advice Letter, supra, pages 17 and 18.)

Therefore, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a financial interest in contracts with SDG&E if he
accepts employment as a contract consultant to SDG&E's Independent Marketing Division, SSC.
As in Roy, his payment source is ultimately SDG&E, and he would have a financial interest in that
he could be influenced by a desire to maintain a favorable ongoing relationship with SDG&E and
foster the prospect of future business opportunities with SSC and SDG&E.

Step Five: Does either a remote interest or a non-interest exception apply?

None of the criteria for a remote interest or non-interest are applicable here. (See Sections
1091 and 1091.5.) Although the Legislature has created a remote exception of a supplier of goods

File No. A-16-263
Page No. 7

or services to the contracting party, it is only applicable if the person has done so for at least five
years prior to appointment to office. (Section 1091b(8).)

As noted above, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. Please note that a decision to
modify, extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under
section 1090. (See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980)103 Cal.App.3d 191.) If Mr. Butkiewicz
accepts a contract consultant position with SCC, the Water Authority would not be able to
participate in the renewal or modification of any existing contract or participate in the making of
any new contracts with SDG&E, unless the rule of necessity applied. We have not analyzed the rule
of necessity as your question does not involve the facts of a particular contract.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility oflosses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson, 
supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fu. 5; Teny 
v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby ( 1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-
432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002). Employees have been found to have a financial 
interest in a contract that involves their employer, even where the contract would not result in a 
change in income or directly involve the employee, because an employee has an overall interest in 
the financial success of the finn and continued employment. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 
(2001).) 
Similar to the financial interest of an employee, past advice letters have found that an 
independent contractor has a financial interest in continuing the business relationship and looking 
for future business opportunities with a contracting finn. (Roy Advice Letter, No. A-16-157, see 
also Khuu Advice Letter, No. 1-14-107.) In Roy, we found that a county Health and Human Services 
Agency public official would have a financial interest in a contract between the county and a 
hospital if she took an independent contractor, temporary physician position, at a medical group. 
Although the medical group would pay her under the contract, the medical group's costs were 
reimbursed by the hospital. Referring to reasoning from past advice letters and Attorney General 
Opinions, the Roy Advice letter found that even where the payment for services came indirectly 
from the hospital, the public official's relationship could be "influenced by a desire to maintain a 
favorable ongoing relationship" with the hospital and "the prospect of future business 
opportunities." (Roy Advice Letter, supra, pages 17 and 18.) 
Therefore, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a financial interest in contracts with SDG&E if he 
accepts employment as a contract consultant to SDG&E's Independent Marketing Division, SSC. 
As in Roy, his payment source is ultimately SDG&E, and he would have a financial interest in that 
he could be influenced by a desire to maintain a favorable ongoing relationship with SDG&E and 
foster the prospect of future business opportunities with SSC and SDG&E. 
Step Five: Does either a remote i11terest or a 11011-illterest exceptio11 apply? 
None of the criteria for a remote interest or non-interest are applicable here. (See Sections 
1091 and 1091.5.) Although the Legislature has created a remote exception of a supplier of goods  
or services to the contracting party, it is only applicable if the person has done so for at least five 
years prior to appointment to office. (Section I 091 (b )(8).) 
As noted above, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. Please note that a decision to 
modify, extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under 
section 1090. (See City of Imperial Beac/1 v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d I 91.) If Mr. Butkiewicz 
accepts a contract consultant position with SCC, the Water Authority would not be able to 
participate in the renewal or modification of any existing contract or participate in the making of 
any new contracts with SDG&E, unless the rule of necessity applied. We have not analyzed the rule 
of necessity as your question does not involve the facts of a particular contract.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I. Under the Act, may Mr. Butkiewicz participate in Water Authority contract decisions 
regarding San Diego Gas & Electric ("SDG&E"), if he accepts employment as a contract consultant 
for SDG&E's independent marketing division, Sempra Services Corporation ("SSC")? 
2. Would Section 1090 prohibit the Water Authority from entering into contracts with 
SDG&E if Mr. Butkiewicz accepts employment as a consultant to SSC?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Under the Act, if Water Authority Board member Jerry Butkiewicz accepts 
employment with SSC, he a conflict of interest and may not participate in the making of any 
contracts with SDG&E. 
2. Yes. In the event he becomes a contract consultant to SSC, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a 
financial interest in contracts with SDG&E under Section 1090, and the Water Authority would be 
barred from making further contracts or renegotiating existing contracts with SDG&E.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 630 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 6, 2017 Rosann Gallien Deputy General Counsel San Diego County Water Authority 4677 Overland Avenue San Diego, CA 92123-1233 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-263 Dear Ms. Gallien: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Jerry Butkiewicz, Board member of the San Diego County Water Authority ("Water Authority"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090, et seq.1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. Nor do we advise under other areas of law that could be affected, including restrictions on incompatible activities. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) The following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. Under the Act, may Mr. Butkiewicz participate in Water Authority contract decisions regarding San Diego Gas & Electric ("SDG&E"), if he accepts employment as a contract consultant for SDG&E's independent marketing division, Sempra Services Corporation ("SSC")? 2. Would Section 1090 prohibit the Water Authority from entering into contracts with SDG&E if Mr. Butkiewicz accepts employment as a consultant to SSC? _________________________ 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-263 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. No. Under the Act, if Water Authority Board member Jerry Butkiewicz accepts employment with SSC, he a conflict of interest and may not participate in the making of any contracts with SDG&E. 2. Yes. In the event he becomes a contract consultant to SSC, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a financial interest in contracts with SDG&E under Section 1090, and the Water Authority would be barred from making further contracts or negotiating existing contracts with SDG&E. FACTS You provided the Public Utility's Draft Resolution E-4874, dated July 14, 2016 for background on the issue of SDG&E's Independent Marketing Division, SSC. In 2011 the Legislature added Public Utilities Code Section 707, which requires that an electrical corporation shall only market against
